01 / 04

PreVisit

Symptom Articulation & Appointment Prep

Patients forget up to 80% of what they planned to say by the time they sit down. PreVisit moves the hard work upstream. It meets people in plain language, structures their account quietly, and hands the doctor something they can actually use.

PlatformiOS + Web
RoleSolo Designer
CategoryPre-clinical
View Prototype
PreVisit
Prepare. Articulate. Be heard.

"The problem is not that patients are unprepared. The problem is that no tool has ever helped them prepare."

80%
Forgotten Before the Room
Research shows patients forget up to 80% of what they planned to say once inside a clinical setting. Anxiety suppresses recall. The design response is structure, not reassurance.
15'
Working Within the Window
The average primary care visit is 15 to 17 minutes. Moving the translation of experiential language into usable clinical language upstream is the core product decision.
The Q
The Design Question
How do you help someone articulate something they do not yet have words for, without leading them toward a conclusion or making them feel like they are self-diagnosing?
Plain
Language-First Design
The app meets users in their own words and structures their account quietly. The output the doctor receives is actionable without erasing the patient's voice.
Gap
An Underserved Moment
PreVisit lives in the space between noticing something is wrong and sitting across from a doctor. That space was completely undesigned before this.
Anxiety
Structure as Relief
Structure reduces anxiety not by telling someone to calm down, but by doing the cognitive work anxiety would otherwise prevent.

01 / The Problem

The clinical appointment is failing before it starts

Every day, patients walk into doctor appointments having rehearsed what to say, only to forget most of it once they sit down. Research consistently shows that patients forget 40 to 80% of what they intended to discuss. The result is a consultation shaped more by what the patient happened to remember than by what actually matters.

This is not a memory problem. It is an anxiety problem. Clinical environments suppress recall. The asymmetry between doctor and patient makes it harder to articulate vague, early-stage concerns. And the visit itself is short: 15 to 17 minutes for a typical primary care appointment. There is no slack in the system for hesitation.

80%
Forgotten by appointment
Patients forget up to 80% of what they planned to say once inside a clinical setting. Anxiety, not carelessness, is the cause.
15′
Average visit window
The average primary care visit is 15 to 17 minutes. There is no room for the patient to find their words in real time.
0
Tools for this moment
No widely adopted tool exists for the specific moment between noticing something is wrong and sitting across from a doctor.

"The problem is not that patients are unprepared. The problem is that no tool has ever helped them prepare."

02 / The Design Question

Articulation without diagnosis

How do you help someone articulate something they do not yet have words for, without leading them toward a conclusion or making them feel like they are self-diagnosing?

This is the core tension PreVisit had to resolve. The user is pre-clinical. They are not a patient yet. They have a felt experience but no medical language for it. A tool that introduces clinical terminology too early risks two failures: false confidence in a wrong conclusion, or escalated anxiety from seeing scary terms attached to vague symptoms.

The design response was to stay in the user's language as long as possible. PreVisit does not translate experiential language into clinical language for the user. It structures their account quietly and hands the doctor something actionable. The translation happens on the clinical side, not the patient side.

03 / Competitive Landscape

Tools that assume the wrong user

A review of Ada Health, WebMD Symptom Checker, and Apple Health revealed a consistent pattern: existing tools are designed for users who already have some medical literacy and are looking for confirmation or next steps. None are designed for the moment of initial uncertainty, when the user does not yet know what to call what they are feeling.

Gap 01

Premature clinical framing

Symptom checkers ask users to select from clinical categories before the user has language for their experience. This forces translation too early and produces either inaccurate inputs or user abandonment.

Gap 02

Self-diagnosis by design

Every existing tool produces a list of possible conditions. For a user already anxious about an unidentified symptom, this amplifies uncertainty rather than reducing it.

Gap 03

No output for the doctor

Even when a user completes a symptom checker, the output is designed for the user, not the clinician. No existing tool produces a structured handoff document that a doctor can use to make the first five minutes of an appointment more productive.

04 / User Context

Three behavioral archetypes

Rather than inventing personas, PreVisit's user context is grounded in published behavioral health research. Three archetypes emerged from the literature, each representing a distinct relationship with pre-clinical uncertainty.

Archetype 01

The Minimizer

Downplays symptoms until they become undeniable

This user rationalizes early symptoms as stress, fatigue, or aging. They delay seeking care because they do not want to "waste the doctor's time." By the time they book an appointment, the window for early intervention may have narrowed.

Core need: Permission and structure to take vague symptoms seriously before they become urgent.

Archetype 02

The Researcher

Googles symptoms extensively before the appointment

This user arrives with a self-constructed narrative built from online sources of varying reliability. They have already anchored on a possible diagnosis and may resist clinical redirection. The appointment becomes a negotiation rather than an examination.

Core need: A structured way to present what they have observed without pre-concluding what it means.

Archetype 03

The Overwhelmed

Knows something is wrong but cannot organize their thoughts

This user feels the weight of multiple overlapping concerns. They cannot distinguish which symptom is most important. Under the time pressure of a short appointment, they default to whatever comes to mind first, which may not be the most clinically relevant concern.

Core need: Cognitive scaffolding that organizes their account before the appointment, so anxiety does not dictate what gets discussed.

05 / Design Decisions

Structure as the intervention

Decision 01

Conversation-first entry, not form-first

The app opens with an open prompt, not a checklist. Users describe what they are experiencing in their own words. The interface then structures that input into categories the user can review and refine. This preserves the patient's voice while quietly organizing their account for clinical use.

Decision 02

No diagnosis output, ever

PreVisit never suggests what the user might have. The output is a structured summary of observations, duration, severity, and impact on daily life. The diagnostic step belongs to the doctor. This was a deliberate constraint that shaped every screen in the app.

Decision 03

Dual output: patient summary and clinician handoff

The user gets a readable summary they can review before the appointment. The doctor gets a structured document formatted for clinical intake. Same data, two presentations. This means the first five minutes of the appointment start from shared ground instead of from scratch.

06 / Reflection

Designing for a moment nobody owned

Design reflection

PreVisit taught me that some of the most impactful design problems are the ones nobody has claimed yet. The space between noticing something is wrong and sitting across from a doctor was completely undesigned. Not because it is unimportant, but because it falls between categories: not quite consumer health, not quite clinical software, not quite a productivity tool. It required holding ambiguity long enough to find the right frame rather than defaulting to the nearest existing category. The parallel to my other work is direct: MedMate addresses the post-clinical space, ClearCare addresses the triage decision, and PreVisit addresses the moment before all of it. Each project started with a question nobody was asking yet.

All work

02 / 04

MedMate

Medication Comprehension & Caregiver Coordination

Half of chronic illness patients do not take their medications as prescribed. Not because they forget. Because they do not understand the medication well enough to feel the cost of stopping. MedMate is built for that gap.

PlatformiOS
RoleSolo Designer
CategoryPost-clinical
View Prototype
MedMate
Medication clarity for caregivers

"The problem is not that people forget their medication. The problem is they do not understand it well enough to feel the cost of stopping."

50%
The Adherence Gap
Half of chronic illness patients do not take medications as prescribed, contributing to 125,000 preventable deaths and up to $300 billion in avoidable costs annually in the US.
For
Caregivers as Primary Users
A significant portion of medication management falls on caregivers, not patients. No existing app was built for them as the primary user.
After
Beyond the Reminder
Existing apps stop at the notification. MedMate addresses what happens after: what this medication does, what to do if you miss a dose, what side effects to watch for.
AI
Comprehension Layer
The AI does not add features. It reduces friction. Clinical information surfaces in plain language at the moment it is needed, not buried in a manual nobody reads.
Drift
Detecting Slip Before Crisis
Adherence breaks gradually. The design detects drift before full breakdown and responds like a conversation, not a warning.
Post
The Post-Clinical Space
MedMate occupies the space that follows diagnosis: the daily, ongoing reality of managing a condition that existing health apps have systematically underserved.

01 / The Problem

The clinical reality of non-adherence

Medication non-adherence is one of the most well-documented and costly failures in modern healthcare. According to the WHO, approximately 50% of patients with chronic illness do not take their medications as prescribed. In the US alone, this contributes to an estimated 125,000 preventable deaths and $100 to $300 billion in avoidable healthcare costs annually.

These are not numbers caused by forgetfulness. Patients stop taking medications because they do not understand why a drug works, because side effects feel worse than the original condition, because their routine breaks once and never recovers, or because nobody in their care loop notices the slip until it becomes a crisis.

The gap existing tools leave open

Reminder apps solve a narrow version of the problem. They fire a notification. They do not tell you what happens if you miss a dose, what that medication is actually doing in your body, which foods to avoid, or what the warning signs of a serious reaction look like. The result is that patients take their meds on a schedule they do not understand and stop the moment any friction enters their lives.

"The problem is not that people forget their medication. The problem is that they do not understand it well enough to feel the cost of stopping."

The caregiver dimension

A significant portion of medication management in chronic illness falls on caregivers, not patients. A parent managing a child with multiple prescriptions, an adult child managing an elderly parent, a spouse navigating a partner's post-surgery regimen. These individuals are operating in someone else's health context, with imperfect information, under emotional pressure. No existing app is designed for them as the primary user.

02 / The Design Question

Beyond the reminder

"How does the app respond when adherence starts slipping, before it fully breaks? And how do we give a caregiver the comprehension they need to manage someone else's medication with confidence?"

This framing intentionally avoids the word "reminder." Reminders are a solved problem. The question is about what happens in the space around the reminder, and the comprehension layer that makes adherence feel meaningful rather than administrative. Two tensions were identified as design anchors: behavioral drift that must be caught before full breakdown, and comprehension that must be delivered in plain language at the moment it is needed.

03 / Competitive Analysis

The same user, everywhere

Every competitor has optimized for the same user: a health-literate adult managing their own medications who already understands what each drug does and needs only to be reminded when to take it. This is a narrow slice of the actual population dealing with chronic medication. It excludes caregivers, excludes newly diagnosed patients, excludes anyone who has ever Googled a medication side effect at midnight because the app gave them nothing.

CapabilityMedisafeMyTherapyApple HealthRoundhealthMedMate
Medication remindersYesYesYesYesYes
Drug-specific missed dose logicNoNoNoNoYes
Plain-language drug explanationNoNoNoNoYes
Side effect triage frameworkNoPartialNoNoYes
Caregiver as primary userNoNoNoNoYes
Adherence slip detectionNoPartialNoPartialYes
What to expect timelineNoNoNoNoYes

"MedMate is not a better reminder app. It is a medication comprehension and caregiver coordination tool."

04 / User Context

Three archetypes from behavioral evidence

Archetype 01

The Caregiver

Managing a parent, partner, or child with multiple prescriptions

Over 53 million Americans provide unpaid care to an adult or child. A significant portion manage prescriptions they did not choose and do not fully understand. The caregiver's relationship with medication is defined by anxiety about getting it wrong, not by habit or routine.

Core need: Comprehension and confidence, not just reminders.

Archetype 02

The Newly Diagnosed Patient

Recently prescribed, unfamiliar with long-term medication management

Patients forget 40 to 80% of the medical information provided during a consultation, and of the information they do retain, approximately half is incorrect. This user leaves the clinic with a prescription and almost no reliable memory of what was discussed.

Core need: A plain-language explanation of what the drug does and what to expect.

Archetype 03

The Chronic Patient in Routine Drift

Long-term user whose habit structure has partially broken down

Routines are most vulnerable after disruption events: travel, illness, change in work schedule, stress. Chronic patients who have managed a regimen successfully for months can lose the habit entirely after a single disruption week. The signal of drift is subtle and no existing app detects it before it becomes full non-adherence.

Core need: Pattern recognition without surveillance. An app that notices drift and opens a conversation, not an alert.

05 / Design Decisions

Every choice was reasoned

Decision 01

Caregiver as the primary user

Competitors assume the patient is the user. MedMate is designed around the caregiver. The primary user manages someone else's medications within their own account. This distinction changes everything from the greeting to the information architecture. The app opens with "Good morning, Sarah" and immediately frames the task as caring for someone else. National Alliance for Caregiving (2020): 53 million unpaid caregivers in the US. None of the top medication apps are designed for this user as primary.

Decision 02

The Understand tab as core navigation

Users Google their medications constantly. They trust Wikipedia over the prescription leaflet because it is readable. Every medication in MedMate has an Understand tab that explains in plain language what the drug does, how it works step by step, and what the user should expect week by week. Comprehension is the foundation of compliance. A user who understands what a medication does and why it works has a reason to keep taking it.

Decision 03

Drift detection that responds like a conversation

Adherence breaks gradually. The design detects the pattern before full breakdown and responds not with an alert but with a check-in. Tone matters as much as content. A missed dose logged at 11pm instead of 8am is the beginning of drift. The app notices this pattern and opens a conversation: "You have been taking this later than usual this week. Is everything okay?" This is not surveillance. It is care.

All work

03 / 04

ClearCare

Telehealth Triage & Care Routing

27% of US ER visits could be handled at urgent care or via telehealth. The barrier is not access to care. It is confidence in the decision to seek it. ClearCare gets users from symptoms to a clear recommendation in under 2 minutes. No login. No anxiety amplified.

PlatformMobile Web
RoleSolo Designer
TypeProduct Concept
View Prototype
ClearCare
Triage in under 2 minutes

"The barrier is not access to care. It is confidence in the decision to seek it."

<2'
Symptoms to Recommendation
From first symptom selection to a confident care recommendation in under 2 minutes. The recommendation appears before any sign-up prompt or upsell.
3
Tier Urgency Routing
Self-care or telehealth, urgent care, or emergency. Each tier has a distinct design intent. The emergency tier is full-screen, unambiguous, and never softened.
0
No Sign-Up Required
Users get their recommendation before any account creation. People abandon health apps the moment they feel the product is extracting from them.
27%
The System Problem
Roughly 27% of US ER visits could be handled at urgent care or via telehealth, costing an estimated $32 billion annually in avoidable emergency spend.
Calm
Tone as a Design Tool
Healthcare anxiety peaks at symptom onset. ClearCare leads with calm. Plain language, no binary outputs, no jargon. The design says: you made the right call.
First
Answer Before Ask
ClearCare's positioning: answer first, earn trust before asking for anything. Most healthcare products have this exactly backwards.

01 / The Problem

A moment of uncertainty nobody designed for

Every day, millions of people face a moment of health uncertainty: Is this bad enough for the ER? Should I wait it out? Can I do a video call? Without guidance, most people either panic and over-utilize emergency services or hesitate and delay necessary care. Both outcomes are costly.

27%
Avoidable ER visits
Roughly 27% of US ER visits could be handled at urgent care or via telehealth.
$32B
Annual avoidable spend
Estimated cost of unnecessary emergency visits to the US healthcare system each year.
<2'
ClearCare's response
Symptoms to clear recommendation in under two minutes, no login required.

Existing solutions fail users in a critical moment. Symptom checkers are long, clinical, and anxiety-inducing. Healthcare portals gate information behind logins. Most apps optimize for booking, not for helping users make the right call first.

"The barrier is not access to care. It is confidence in the decision to seek it."

02 / Research & Competitive Landscape

Three gaps, consistently

Secondary research revealed a consistent pattern: healthcare anxiety peaks at the moment of symptom onset, not at the point of booking. Users described feeling overwhelmed by medical language, afraid of wasting a doctor's time, and unsure whether their symptoms were "serious enough." A competitive review of WebMD Symptom Checker, Ada Health, and Apple Health surfaced three recurring gaps.

Gap 01

Recommendation buried behind friction

Existing tools hide the recommendation behind 20+ questions or a login wall. Users abandon the flow before they get an answer. ClearCare's response: the recommendation appears first. Trust is earned in the first 90 seconds before anything is asked of the user.

Gap 02

Binary outputs with no urgency nuance

Most tools route to one of two outcomes: "see a doctor" or "you are fine." Neither helps a user understand the urgency of their specific situation. ClearCare implements genuine three-tier triage: self-care or telehealth, urgent care, or emergency. Each tier has a distinct design intent and a distinct tone.

Gap 03

Tone that amplifies anxiety

Clinical language in a moment of health uncertainty makes anxiety worse, not better. ClearCare leads with calm. Plain language, no jargon, no binary alarm. The design says: you made the right call coming here. Even the emergency tier is clear without being catastrophizing.

03 / Design Decisions

Answer first. Earn trust before asking for anything.

Decision 01

Recommendation-first flow

The single most deliberate structural choice in ClearCare is that the recommendation appears before any sign-up prompt, upsell, or account creation. This was a direct response to a pattern observed across competitors: users abandon health apps the moment they feel manipulated. The flow is intentionally linear: select symptoms, get routed, then optionally book.

Decision 02

Three-tier urgency routing

Self-care or telehealth validates the user's instinct to wait and reduces unnecessary in-person volume. Urgent care converts anxious hesitators into confident same-day patients. Emergency is a safety-first override: never buried, never softened. Chest pain or respiratory distress triggers an immediate full-screen alert. The design says: stop reading, act now.

Decision 03

Zero sign-up to get an answer

Users get their recommendation before any account creation. A direct response to a pattern across competitors: people abandon health apps the moment they feel the product is extracting from them. ClearCare's positioning is to answer first, earn trust before asking for anything. The recommendation is the product's most important moment and it cannot be gated.

04 / Reflection

What comes next

ClearCare is a concept-stage product. A natural next phase would explore what happens after the moment of triage. Follow-up nudges to close the loop on whether the user acted on the recommendation. A provider-side dashboard for clinics to see inbound triage data and manage same-day capacity. Deeper accessibility testing, especially with elderly users who were a core target audience. And an integration layer connecting to real urgent care wait times and telehealth booking APIs.

"The north star metric: did the user leave with a clear, confident next step, and did that step match the clinical recommendation?"

All work

04 / 04

HealthHacks

UX Strategy & Brand System

A solo brand build for Prosthex's first public event. A health hackathon that needed to speak to engineers and clinicians in the same breath. The visual language was an audience decision, not a stylistic one. 70 to 80k views in 30 days, 100+ participants, zero brand precedent.

TypeBrand Identity
RoleSolo Brand Lead
PlatformsIG + LinkedIn + Discord

"The visual language of the category was producing the wrong audience before a single word was read."

80k
Views in 30 Days
Across Instagram, LinkedIn, and Discord for a first-time event with no prior public presence and zero paid promotion.
100+
Cross-Discipline Participants
Engineers, pre-med students, and early health founders in the same room. The brand's core job was getting all three audiences to show up.
The Q
The Strategic Problem
Health hackathons have no established visual language. The closest reference, major CS hackathons, signals "engineers only" through dark/neon cyberpunk aesthetics.
Solo
End-to-End Brand Build
Strategy, identity, messaging hierarchy, brandmark, social templates, Discord architecture, and LinkedIn positioning. All solo, from a founder conversation that had no brief.
Bridge
The DNA Helix Brandmark
An abstract mark that reads as both biological and technical. Recognizable to engineers and clinicians simultaneously without committing to either register.
Trust
Clarity Before Energy
Most hackathon communications lead with excitement and bury the practical information. HealthHacks reversed this: reduce friction first, build excitement second.

01 / The Brief That Was Not One

Excavating an inarticulate vision

Most design projects begin with a brief. This one began with a conversation with a founder who knew exactly what she wanted but could not yet put it into words. Prosthex's mission was still evolving at the time, and HealthHacks, as its first public-facing initiative, needed to represent something credible without over-committing to a specific mission statement.

The working brief I constructed: build a communication system that makes HealthHacks feel serious, specific, and worth showing up for, to people who have never heard of a health hackathon before. Constraints: solo, zero brand precedent, a tight timeline, and a founder whose feedback style was directional rather than prescriptive. Every decision had to be both grounded in research and defensible on instinct.

"Excavating a founder's unarticulate vision through conversation is a core design skill, directly parallel to the symptom articulation work in PreVisit."

02 / The Strategic Problem

The visual language was producing the wrong audience

Health-focused hackathons are rare. There is no established visual or cultural language for them. The closest references (HackMIT, HackHarvard, major university hackathons) share a dominant aesthetic: dark backgrounds, neon accents, circuit-board motifs, aggressive typography. Cyberpunk by default. That aesthetic is not neutral. It is a cultural signal that says: this event is for engineers who identify with hacker culture.

HealthHacks needed three types of people in the room: engineers and builders, but also people from clinical and biological backgrounds, and early founders working on health problems. That second and third audience would look at a HackMIT poster and feel, instinctively, that the event was not for them.

"The visual language of the category was producing the wrong audience before a single word was read."

The strategic problem was not differentiation for its own sake. It was audience legibility. The brand had to signal technical credibility without signaling cultural exclusivity.

03 / Research & Insight

Three friction points, consistently

I audited the visual and messaging systems of major hackathons (HackMIT, HackHarvard, PennApps, and several others), mapping their aesthetic choices, tone of voice, and how they framed participation. The pattern was consistent: dark palettes, glitch-inspired graphics, language that assumed technical fluency from the first line of copy. None of them were health-focused. None had to speak to someone with a clinical background.

Through interviews with potential applicants (engineering students, pre-med students, and early founders), three friction points emerged consistently.

Friction 01

Intimidation

The hackathon format itself felt exclusionary to anyone without a strong CS background. The word "hackathon" carries baggage. People with clinical or biological backgrounds did not see themselves in the existing visual language of the category.

Friction 02

Ambiguity around tracks

People did not know what they were actually being asked to build, or what the bar for participation was. The lack of specific track descriptions and outcome clarity made it hard to commit to applying.

Friction 03

Low credibility signals

For a first-time event from an organization most people had not heard of, there was little in the existing communication to answer: "Why should I trust this is worth my time?" Credibility had to be built visually and tonally from nothing.

"The dominant hackathon aesthetic was solving for the wrong problem. It optimized for excitement among an already-converted audience. HealthHacks needed to reduce friction for a skeptical, cross-disciplinary audience first, and create excitement second."

04 / Positioning & Messaging

Clarity before energy

HealthHacks would not compete on energy. It would compete on clarity and credibility. On Instagram, posts were structured to answer the three friction questions directly: what the tracks were, who the event was for, and what participants could expect in terms of mentorship, judging, and the pitch pathway. On Discord, the focus shifted to reducing ambiguity with clear channel structure and specific track descriptions. On LinkedIn, the tone was more formal and credibility signals (mentors, judges, organizational backing) were foregrounded.

The messaging hierarchy: reduce friction first, build excitement second. Most hackathon communications reverse this. They lead with energy and bury the practical information. For a new, cross-disciplinary event, that is exactly backwards.

05 / Identity & Brand System

A legibility decision, not a stylistic one

The visual identity had to do one thing above all else: signal that this was a health event, not a CS event, without looking clinical or sterile. The palette (deep teal, warm olive, sage, and cream on near-black) was chosen for exactly this reason. It reads as organic and considered rather than aggressive or cold. It has the seriousness of a professional event without the intimidation of dark-mode tech culture.

Brandmark

The DNA helix as a bridge

The helix motif bridges the two core audiences. It is recognizable to both the engineering and biological sides of the room. Rendered as an abstract, gestural mark rather than a literal illustration, it stays flexible across contexts and scales down cleanly to a submark for smaller applications.

Typography

Authoritative, not aggressive

Impact for display headings and Franklin Gothic Demi Condensed for supporting text. Both are bold and event-forward but read as authoritative rather than aggressive. The choice avoided the rounded, friendly typography of consumer health apps (too soft) and the aggressive, condensed grotesques of tech hackathons (too exclusive).

System

Scalable across all platforms

The system was designed to scale across Instagram posts, stories, Discord banners, and LinkedIn headers without requiring bespoke treatment for each format. Color ratios, type hierarchy, and brandmark usage rules were defined so that the system could be applied consistently across any new touchpoint.

06 / Outcomes & Reflection

The brand solved the problem it was designed to solve

80k
Views in 30 days
Across Instagram, LinkedIn, and Discord for a first-time event with no prior public presence.
100+
Participants
Across neurotech, diagnostics, clinical technology, and open health tracks.
10+
Winners
Across hackathon and pitch competition tracks. Mixed engineering and clinical backgrounds in the room.

Reflection

The most important thing this project taught me is that ambiguity at the brief stage is not a problem to solve before design begins. It is a design problem in itself. The work of excavating an inarticulate vision, translating it into defensible decisions, and building a system that holds even when the organization's direction is still in motion is exactly the work. The parallel to PreVisit is not coincidental. In both cases, the design job was to make the implicit legible. What I would do differently: establish clearer feedback loops earlier. The directional feedback style was manageable because of the closeness of the working relationship, but on a larger team or with a more formal client, that ambiguity would need to be structured out of the process more deliberately.

Studio Practice

Paint, dance & studio art

Classical Indian miniature painting, Bharatanatyam dance, gouache, oil, collage. Both practices run parallel to the design work and ask the same thing: learn to see clearly, then make something someone else can feel.

Bharatanatyam & classical dance

Trained Bharatanatyam dancer. The discipline runs deeper than performance. It trains precision in gesture, intentionality in expression, and the ability to hold someone's attention through structure and rhythm. The same instincts show up in how I approach interface choreography and information flow.

Manushi Rathod © 2026 Designed with curiosity. Built with care. imanushirathod@gmail.com

About me

Manushi
Rathod

Designer, researcher, painter.

Multidisciplinary by nature. I have a background in biomedical sciences and studio art, which means I came to design through two very different kinds of rigour. One trains you to look for evidence. The other trains you to look, full stop.

My healthcare design work follows a single thread through the patient journey: PreVisit for the moment you notice something is wrong, ClearCare for the moment you need to decide what to do, and MedMate for the ongoing reality of living with a diagnosis. Each one started with a question nobody had thought to answer yet.

Outside of screens, I paint and dance. Mostly Indian classical miniature painting and mixed media collage, and Bharatanatyam classical dance. Both practices ask the same question: learn to see clearly, then make something someone else can feel.

Based in Florida. Open to work. Always curious.
